schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch lay out the chapter's four registers and note the Gershon/Gershom variant that betrays the compiler's several sources.
Gill frames the design: kingdom to Judah, birthright to Joseph, priesthood to Levi, whose posterity the historian now unfolds.
Henry: priests were most concerned to prove their pedigree, since all their office hung on it (cf. Ezra 2:62-63); yet only the great High Priest can make atonement.
Clarke finds the fuller, more correct genealogy of Levi a proof the author was himself a priest.

themes: the four registers of the chapter

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the sons of Kohath as the doorway to the high-priestly succession — twenty-two generations, 'he begat' repeated, Phinehas already priest in the judges' day (Judg. 20:28).
Gill notes the four sons stand in the same order as the law, content to let the older books supply what is abridged.

themes: chronology from Aaron to the temple

schools.summary: Gill marks Aaron the high priest, Moses the prophet and lawgiver, Miriam the prophetess — memorable persons kept here for descent.
Henry reveres Moses and Aaron as instruments of deliverance and figures of Christ, and will not pass Nadab and Abihu without recalling the strange fire that made them monuments of divine justice.

themes: prophet and priest as types of Christ

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch set out the six lines of descent this section unfolds, and mark again the Gershon/Gershom variant as a token of the compiler's multiple documents.
Gill: the sons of Levi are named a second time for the sake of their posterity, given in the verses that follow.
Henry finds no confusion in the doubling but the method of a careful register, naming the heads afresh before tracing each branch.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott trace Libni's line through seven generations to the unknown Jeaterai (cf. Num. 3:21), noting Jahath, Zimmah, and Zerah recur in Asaph's line; Ellicott would read Jeaterai as 'the Ithrite' (1 Chron. 11:40).
Gill follows the descent, noting the double names (Jahath/Jehiel, Joah/Ethan, Iddo/Adaiah) and that Shimei's branch is passed over.
Keil and Delitzsch call the Kohathite list a tangle of three fragments, and find the head strange — Amminadab where the law has Izhar (Exod. 6:18-24).
Gill draws the long line out for Samuel's sake, identifying Amminadab with Izhar and harmonising the variant names into one chain.
Keil and Delitzsch note the three Levite lists alike in plan but unequal in length, the Merarite reaching only to Asaiah — a mark of how partial the records were.
Gill gives the plainest branch: Mushi's line omitted, only Mahli's short descent to Asaiah set down.

themes: the root of Ethan's share in the song

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott fix the idiom 'to the hands of song' (cf. 1 Chron. 25:2-3; Ps. 123:2), and note the 'house of the Lord' was still David's tent on Zion (2 Sam. 6:17).
Henry: David the sweet psalmist appointed the singing after the ark had rest; the harps once hung on the willows are resumed, a foretaste of the everlasting songs above.
Gill marks where the account of the singers begins.
Clarke brings the Targum: David set them over the singing when the ark was brought in from Obed-edom's house.

Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch fix the setting — ministering before the Tent of Meeting (once at Shiloh, Nob, Gibeon; 2 Chron. 1:3) until Solomon built the house — and the choir's shape: Heman centre, Asaph right, Ethan left.
Gill: they ministered with psalms before the ark in the tent David pitched, the Targum's 'tabernacle of time.'
Clarke notes the order is the one specified in the verses that follow.

themes: ministry of song before the tent

schools.summary: Ellicott resumes the suspended sentence: the three chiefs Heman, Asaph, and Ethan with their guild-'children' (cf. 1 Chron. 25:1-7); Heman the minstrel, first among equals, grandson of Samuel — a link of music and prophecy (2 Kings 3:15; 1 Sam. 10:5-6).
Gill names the three heads, one of each Levite family, who officiated with their posterity.
Henry: Heman, Samuel's grandson, did not think it below him to be a precentor, as David to be a doorkeeper.

Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch set Asaph as Heman's brother in Levi and in office, of Gershom's younger line, his pedigree partly anticipated in the earlier Gershonite list (Jahath, Zimmah, Zerah; Adaiah/Iddo; Ethni/Jeaterai).
Henry: Asaph was singer and seer both, several psalms bearing his name.
Gill calls him Heman's brother as fellow-descendant of Levi and fellow chief singer.
Clarke judges their singing a synagogue-like chant and denies God appointed the instruments.

outliers.note: Turns the notice of the temple singers into a polemic against instrumental music: God appointed no instruments (the silver trumpets excepted), and instruments in the house of God are, under the Gospel, repugnant to the spirit of Christianity and tend to corrupt worship.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place Ethan in the younger Merarite line of Mushi and note his father's name varies — Kishi here, Kushaiah in the parallel (1 Chron. 15:17).
Wesley identifies Ethan with Jeduthun (1 Chron. 9:16), the name borne in several psalm-titles.
Henry traces Ethan up to Levi and raises the open question whether he and Heman are the Ezrahites of Ps. 88 and Ps. 89.
Gill introduces Ethan with his Merarite brethren, of the same ancestor Levi.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch press the key word 'given' (nethunim), the Levites made over to Aaron's sons for every service of the dwelling (Num. 3:9; 8:16-19).
Henry lists the humbler labours — water, fuel, washing, the sacrifices — and draws the lesson that those who cannot sing may yet serve: as every one has received the gift, so let him minister.
Gill: the Levites not skilled in song, appointed to the other work of God's house.

Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch set out the priests' three offices — the altars, the most holy place, atonement — all 'according to all Moses commanded'; Ellicott dwells on Moses as 'servant of God' (Deut. 34:5; Heb. 3:5), a type of the servant of Isaiah.
Henry: the priests made atonement, ordained for men not themselves, yet subject to God's command; singers, servants, and priests each needed the others.
Gill: Aaron and his sons, of the same tribe yet all priests, set apart for the altar.

Henry calls the Levite cities 'castles' — under the special care of providence — and reads the whole grant as fulfilling Jacob's word that Levi be scattered (Gen. 49:7), diffusing the law, and maintaining the ministers.
Clarke and Wesley agree the priests drew the first lot, Judah's ground yielding their cities (Josh. 21:10).
Keil and Delitzsch read the opening as a superscription over the whole catalogue, a near-repetition of Joshua with 'first' perhaps dropped.
Gill flags two cities lost by careless copying — Juttah and Gibeon — the sum thirteen but only eleven named.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the whole descent from Abishua to Jehozadak, a true succession of high priests, ending in the exile whose captive Jehozadak fathered the returning Joshua (Ezra 3:2; Hag. 1:1).
Henry reads the two ends as one stroke: Eleazar out of Egypt's bondage, Jehozadak into Babylon's — a priesthood that made nothing perfect, waiting for a better hope.
Keil and Delitzsch describe the section's make — the grandsons of Levi enumerated, then three parallel lists each linked by 'his son.'
Gill: the sons of Levi are repeated for the sake of these grandsons, whose names the next verses supply.
Henry recalls how large these names — Gershom, Kohath, Merari — stood in the book of Numbers, where the Levite families were marshalled to their charges.
Keil and Delitzsch treat this as one of the three Kohathite fragments, comparing it link by link with Heman's line and taking Ahimoth for another form of Mahath.
Gill smooths the tangle by his equations — Uriel/Zephaniah, Uzziah/Azariah, Shaul/Joel — so the chain runs unbroken toward Samuel.

Ellicott strikes the doubled Elkanah as an intrusion (from 1 Sam. 1:1) and matches the by-forms — Zophai/Zuph, Nahath/Toah, Eliab/Eliel, Mahath/Ahimoth — so the ascending and descending lists coincide; Keil and Delitzsch reach the same conclusion.

themes: a knot in the Kohathite list
one line preserved in two shapes

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch trace Heman's ascent through Kohath to Levi and Israel, and Ellicott shows the ascending and descending lists identical, their variants betraying different documents.
Henry: Heman, grandson of Samuel and son of the wayward Joel (1 Sam. 8:2-3), shows the blessing leaping a faithless link to rest on a faithful heir.

Keil and Delitzsch carry Asaph's ascent to Gershom and Levi, and Ellicott shows three of its names (Jahath, Zimmah, Zerah), with Adaiah/Iddo and Ethni/Jeaterai, already anticipated in the shorter Gershonite list.
Keil and Delitzsch trace Ethan's ascent, shortest of the three, up through Mahli and Mushi to Merari and Levi.
Henry marks that Ethan too is traced as high as Levi, as truly of the sacred tribe as his fellows.
Wesley's identification of Ethan with Jeduthun gives the bare ascent a living psalmody.

themes: the song a charge on Levi

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read this as a second, briefer roll of Aaron's sons to Ahimaaz, cast like the family-lists and set as a bridge to the register of cities.
Clarke takes the doubling as proof the writer had several tables before him, giving the same descent twice.
Keil and Delitzsch align Hebron with the parallel (Josh. 21:11-12); Ellicott notes the variant names ('city of Arba,' 'hill-country') and that the 'suburbs' are the measured pastures of Num. 35:3-5.
Keil and Delitzsch mark that the city's fields went to Caleb, and correct the mistaken plural 'cities of refuge' (only Hebron) and the dropped suburb-clause; Ellicott adds Joshua's word 'possession.'

themes: town and pasture to the Levites

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott name the eleven-for-thirteen shortfall (Juttah and Gibeon lost) and the variant forms (Hilen/Holon, Ashan/Ain, Alemeth/Almon); Debir means 'oracle,' anciently Kirjath-sepher.
Henry: it does no hurt to Scripture's credit that places be spelt otherwise than in Joshua, since cities have several names and time changes names.

themes: a true record in its blemishes

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch strike the totals (thirteen to Gershom, twelve to Merari) and note two summary verses displaced from their proper place (Josh. 21:8-9); Ellicott reads 'Gershom' and 'half-tribe' against the parallel.

themes: Levi's inheritance the gift of the tribes

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch correct a slip of the preposition and note a whole line dropped, reading the verse as introducing the remaining Kohathites' cities (Josh. 21:20-26).
Clarke suggests each family gave its own name to the city that fell to its lot.

themes: the lot from Judah, Simeon, Benjamin

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch enumerate the Kohathite and Gershonite cities (Josh. 21:27-33), flagging corruptions (Kishion, Jarmuth); Ellicott corrects the opening clause and the loose plural, Gezer being no city of refuge.

themes: towns of weight given to Levi

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch follow the northern cities into Zebulun, marking only two named where Joshua has four (Josh. 21:34-37).
Clarke defends the barren register: as barren rocks are needful parts of the globe, so these tables are needful to the history of grace, not to be despised.
Henry: the scattering of Levi (Gen. 49:7) reached Galilee and Jezreel too, that the law's light might touch the farthest tribes.

themes: the worth of a dry register
light in every room of Israel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch trace the Merarite cities beyond Jordan — Bezer the refuge (Deut. 4:43), Jahzah and its fellows (Josh. 13:18) — verses nearly lost from the copies of Joshua.
Clarke's defence of the barren tables applies: even these remote, half-lost cities were kept, none of Levi's inheritance let fall.
Henry: the scattering reached east of Jordan too, that Reuben and Gad might have God's ministers among them.

themes: cities nearly lost from the text
the ministry at the land's edge

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch carry the eastern cities on — Ramoth in Gilead the refuge (Josh. 21:36), Mahanaim, Heshbon, Jazer (Josh. 13:26) — places of real consequence.
Clarke's word over the whole catalogue stands: barren of interest, yet a needful part of the history of grace, not to be held cheap.
Keil and Delitzsch set down the last pair, Heshbon and Jazer, closing the catalogue of forty-eight cities and the whole genealogy of Levi.
Clarke's reflection is the fittest close: the seemingly driest register is a testimony to God's faithfulness, not to be despised by any who fear him.
Henry gathers the design — Jacob's prophecy fulfilled (Gen. 49:7), the law diffused, the ministers maintained, every tribe benefiting and so contributing.
Clarke: the priesthood continuing so long in Eleazar's family, the writer confines himself to it, omitting Nadab, Abihu, and Ithamar's house.
Keil and Delitzsch: the enumeration communicates the succession of high priests, Phinehas already in office in the judges' day (Judg. 20:28; cf. Num. 25:11).
Henry sets the line's covenant meaning, from Eleazar out of Egypt toward the exile.

themes: the office not merely the family

schools.summary: Clarke: through this Zadok the high priesthood came again into the family of Eleazar.
Henry tells the story presupposed: the dignity had passed to Ithamar's line (Eli's), and in Zadok returned to the right line.
Keil and Delitzsch read the name as one more link in the succession from Eleazar toward the captivity.

themes: the priesthood restored to Eleazar's line

schools.summary: Henry takes Azariah for the priest who withstood Uzziah's invasion of the office (2 Chron. 26:17-18), and contrasts Urijah, blotted from the roll for building Ahaz an idolatrous altar.

Clarke (taking Johanan for Jehoiada) and Wesley refer 'executing the office' to Azariah's worthy, valiant discharge in Uzziah's day; Wesley notes 'the temple Solomon built' distinguishes it from the second temple.

debates.question: To which Azariah does 'he executed the priest's office in the temple Solomon built' belong?

sides.position: to this Azariah, who bravely withstood King Uzziah's presumption
rather to his grandfather Azariah the son of Ahimaaz, who first officiated in Solomon's temple

schools.summary: Clarke: this Seraiah was put to death by Nebuchadnezzar (2 Kings 25:18, 21) when the city fell.
Keil and Delitzsch: Seraiah begat Jehozadak, who went into exile — the father slain, the son carried off.
Henry: the line begun with Eleazar out of Egypt ends with Jehozadak into Babylon, Seraiah the hinge of that grim symmetry.

themes: the last priest of Solomon's temple
the seed of restoration carried alive

schools.summary: Clarke sends the reader to the history of Korah's rebellion (Num. 16).
Keil and Delitzsch find the list's head strange — Amminadab where the law has Izhar begetting Korah (Exod. 6:18-21) — and incline to equate the two.
Gill had already taken Amminadab for Izhar, carrying the line on through Korah and Assir toward Samuel.

themes: a line of rebellion turned to praise
the sons of Korah in the psalms

schools.summary: Clarke calls it a great mistake: Joel has fallen out and vesheni ('and the second') been misread as a name, Vashni; the sons who judged badly and provoked the demand for a king (1 Sam. 8:1-2).
Ellicott agrees Vashni is corrupt, restores 'Joel the firstborn, and the second Abiah,' and cautions (comparing Ruth 4:18-22) that Hebrew tables did not supply every link.
Gill harmonises instead, keeping Vashni as the firstborn's name, also called Joel, as the Syriac and Arabic read.

debates.question: Is 'Vashni' a proper name or a corruption?

sides.position: a corruption of 'and the second,' the name Joel having dropped out
the firstborn's own name, also called Joel

schools.summary: Clarke: this second, shorter list of Aaron's sons proves the writer had several tables before him.
Keil and Delitzsch: a catalogue of high priests from Eleazar to Ahimaaz son of Zadok (2 Sam. 15:27), set as a transition to the register of cities.

themes: the second list of Aaron's sons

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch name the lost towns — Gibeon (confused with Geba) and Juttah — that bring the eleven back to thirteen; Ellicott takes Alemeth/Almon for two valid names of one place.
Clarke: the total is thirteen though only eleven are reckoned, no version supplying the full count.
Clarke notes two cities wanting — Eltekeh and Gibethon (Josh. 21:23).
Ellicott spells out the lacuna (the whole tribe of Dan and two towns dropped) and weighs the cause, preferring corruption to any pious censorship.

debates.question: Why is the tribe of Dan missing from the list?

sides.position: the Chronicler omitted Dan from religious prejudice, the city being the seat of an illicit worship
the omission is due to imperfect sources or careless copyists, not the historian

schools.summary: Clarke turns the reader's impatience into reverence: barren as the register seems, these tables are needful parts of the history of God's providence and grace, not to be despised by any who fear him.
Keil and Delitzsch supply the matter — Golan and Ashtaroth given the Gershonites out of half-Manasseh — their labour of alignment answering the charge of dryness.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch weigh the doubled Kohathite names and incline to identity — Uzziah = Azariah, Uriel = Zephaniah, Shaul = Joel — one house set out twice, downward and upward.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read the fresh 'sons of Elkanah' as the same line resumed, not a new family, Ahimoth being another form of Mahath and the posterity nearly identical with Heman's.

Ellicott notes the one full ascent to Israel serves for all three singers, the names identical with the earlier list though the variants betray different documents; Keil and Delitzsch trace Heman's line to Kohath, Levi, and Israel.

themes: son of Levi, son of Israel
the song rooted in the people

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch correct the plural 'cities of refuge' (only Hebron among these; the other five refuges elsewhere, Josh. 20:7-8) and the suburb-clause dropped after Jattir.

Ellicott plays on Ashan ('smoke') and Ain ('fountain') as two names of one place, notes Juttah dropped (Josh. 21:16) and Joshua's omitted sum of nine cities; Keil and Delitzsch take Ain the more correct form.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch find the text mutilated — Ephraim and Dan dropped, a doubled 'half of Manasseh' left — and restore the sense from Joshua 21:5, the number ten standing firm.

Ellicott notes this verse stands word for word as Joshua 21:7 (omitting 'by lot'); Keil and Delitzsch give the substance — twelve Merarite cities out of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun, the lot God's disposing.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch find the wording barely intelligible and restore it from Joshua 21:20 — the cities of the Kohathite families' border were of Ephraim, the notice abridged and broken.
Wesley glosses 'coasts' as 'borders,' the country within their bounds.

Ellicott prefers Jokmeam to Joshua's Kibzaim and marks the fitness that Bethhoron and Aijalon, scenes of the overthrow of the five Amorite kings (Josh. 10), were given the sacred tribe; Keil and Delitzsch list the same Ephraimite cities.

Ellicott shows two men's names crept in for towns — Aner (Gen. 14:13) for Taanach, Bileam (Balaam) for Ibleam (Josh. 17:11) — with Gath-rimmon a repetition; Keil and Delitzsch set the same cities against Joshua 21:25.

Keil and Delitzsch count this pair among the two-and-twenty generations, a genuine succession marked by the repeated 'begat'; the recurrence of names like Amariah and Ahitub is no confusion but the way of the priestly house.
Henry: the line runs from Eleazar to Jehozadak, and not every priest served with honour — some, like Urijah, were struck from the roll for their sins.

themes: a middle link in the priestly line

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch climb Heman's line through these links; Ellicott matches the by-forms (Zophai/Zuph, Mahath/Ahimoth) so the ascending and descending series coincide, securing Samuel's house behind its singer.
Keil and Delitzsch trace Asaph's ascent through these names to Gershom; Ellicott shows Adaiah/Iddo and Ethni/Jeaterai overlapping the shorter Gershonite list, one descent told twice.
Keil and Delitzsch bring Ethan's line home to Merari and Levi, then turn to the other Levites given for every service of God's house.
Henry marks that Ethan too is traced as high as Levi, as truly of the tribe as his fellows.
Wesley's word that Ethan is Jeduthun gives the bare ascent a remembered psalmody.

themes: the youngest house joined to the song

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the second list ends at Ahimaaz son of Zadok (2 Sam. 15:27), who succeeded his father in Solomon's day — a hinge to the register of cities.
Clarke: the briefer roll bespeaks a compiler working from several tables.

themes: the second list ends at Ahimaaz

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch mark the Merarite cities of Zebulun — only two named where Joshua has four (Josh. 21:34-37), the sons of Merari the last of the Levites numbered.
Clarke's defence of the barren tables bears on the verse: a needful part of the history of grace, not to be lightly esteemed.

themes: no house of Levi left unplaced
